g.data-narration: Welcome to Swing Components and Containers. This lecture covers the fundamental building blocks of Java GUI applications.
g.text: Components and Containers in Swing GUI

g.data-narration: Swing is Java's GUI toolkit providing rich visual components. It uses a lightweight architecture for cross-platform consistent appearance.
g.text: A GUI widget toolkit for Java providing a rich set of components for building user interfaces.
Part of Java Foundation Classes (JFC) | Lightweight components | Pluggable Look and Feel

g.data-narration: Understanding the difference between components and containers is essential. Components are UI elements, containers hold and organize them.
g.text: They form the structural foundation of every Swing GUI application.

g.data-narration: Components are visual UI elements that can receive user input. All Swing components inherit from JComponent class.
g.text: Any object that can be displayed on screen and interact with user. Inherit from JComponent.
Key Characteristics: Visual representation | Receives user input | Can be placed in containers | Has size, position, visibility

g.data-narration: Swing provides numerous components: buttons, labels, text fields, checkboxes, tables, trees, and more for building complete UIs.
Containers are special components that hold other components. They manage layout, positioning, and sizing of their children.
g.text: Special components that can contain other components. They manage layout, positioning, and sizing.

g.data-narration: Top-level containers are the foundation: JFrame for windows, JDialog for dialogs, JWindow for borderless windows. They cannot be contained in other containers.
g.text: The fundamental containers that provide the foundation for Swing applications. Cannot be contained in other containers.

g.data-narration: Intermediate containers organize UI within top-level containers: JPanel for grouping, JScrollPane for scrolling, JSplitPane for dividers, JTabbedPane for tabs.
g.text: Placed within top-level containers to help organize components.

g.data-narration: The component hierarchy shows the containment tree: JFrame contains ContentPane, which contains intermediate containers and components.
Every top-level container has a content pane that holds visible components. Use getContentPane() or add() directly to add components.
g.text: Every top-level container has a content pane that holds visible components.

g.data-narration: Layout managers control component positioning: BorderLayout divides into 5 regions, FlowLayout arranges in rows, GridLayout creates equal-sized grid cells.
g.text: Control positioning and sizing of components within containers.

g.data-narration: Swing components are lightweight (no native peers, better performance, pluggable look and feel) vs AWT's heavyweight components.
All components share methods: setVisible(), setEnabled(), setSize(), setLocation(), setBackground(), setFont(), setToolTipText().
g.text: All components share these manipulation methods.

g.data-narration: A practical login form example: JFrame with JPanel using GridLayout, containing labels, text fields, and a button.
